insight: Giving efficient build-time attackers to non-red colors is dangerous because when a non-red color can ramp attack quickly, it often becomes unbeatable. Red's identity as the fast-attack color provides a natural balance check that other colors lack.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of Magna Kronus design and why non-red efficient early attackers are problematic for game balance.

insight: Magna Kronus (10GGBBDD, 14 HP, buildtime 2) generates 3 attack per turn passively, with a click ability granting 5 extra damage exhausting for 3 turns. In practice it attacks 8 on the first click then 3/3/3 thereafter, analogous to how Drake attacks 4 then 2/2/2. Clicking is marginal once you have a large stack since 3 attackers doing 7/turn beats 22 every 3 turns.
author: masn6811
context: Live playtesting and balance discussion of the new Magna Kronus unit during the November 2020 test event.

insight: For large units, even a 1-gold cost increase can render them unplayable, as demonstrated when Drake's cost went from 16BBB to 17BBB and it became essentially unviable. This means balance adjustments to big units require more careful calibration than similar adjustments to small units.
author: masn6811
context: Debate over how much to nerf Magna Kronus, with masn cautioning against over-nerfing by referencing Drake's history.

insight: The Magna Kronus click ability is primarily valuable for denying absorb early, not for raw damage output. Once you have a large enough stack that you are not clicking, the passive 3-per-turn attack is more efficient than the click pattern, making it a gambit-style unit in late game.
author: masn6811
context: Post-event analysis of Magna Kronus gameplay after the November 2020 test event.

insight: Infusion Grid counters Magna Kronus effectively because Magna is exactly the kind of unit that forces the opponent to acquire soak within 2-3 turns, and infusion grids can be purchased in response before the next Magna click becomes relevant.
author: velizar_
context: Post-game analysis after masn lost a Magna Kronus game to Tarsiers during the test event.

insight: Scorchilla is considered slightly too strong at its current cost, but even 5GR buildtime 2 may be too heavy a nerf. The preferred adjustment direction from masn and wonderboat is reducing it to 2 HP rather than increasing its gold cost.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of balance changes for the Friday test event, responding to a suggestion to increase Scorchilla's color cost.

insight: Thorium Dynamo nerf to 6EDD felt like less of an auto-buy while keeping smooth opening lines (DD/DD, DD/D1, 11/D1), but the unit remained very strong and slightly too good in Blood Pact because HP was not also reduced. masn originally suggested 6 HP, with 7 HP potentially also acceptable.
author: masn6811
context: Post-event assessment of Thorium Dynamo after the November 2020 test event.

insight: The Smorcus nerf from 5RR to 6RR (with click cost changing to 1R) smoothed gameplay lines and hurt the Smorcus rush and 4-attack transition into economy, but is almost 0 net gold difference when used for pure value because two Smorcuses can still threaten one Animus worth of attack.
author: masn6811
context: Post-event assessment of Smorcus balance after the November 2020 test event, debating the size of the nerf with velizar_.

insight: Grimbotch (Botch) nerf reducing its lifespan by 1 was considered better than the alternative 1 HP nerf because the lifespan version fits elegantly with Blood Pact and is actually stronger than 1 HP Botch, though the LS3 version was seen as underwhelming by some testers.
author: silentslayers
context: Post-event discussion of Botch nerf options, responding to sano's question about 1 HP vs lifespan nerf.

insight: Units that are always-buys or never-buys reduce strategic options rather than expanding them. An auto-buy unit does not create more choices; it removes the buy decision. Conversely, a unit that is a never-buy in most sets is equally problematic for the same reason.
author: masn6811
context: masn explaining his balance philosophy to elyot in response to elyot's characterization of masn's preferences.

insight: A unit assigned Legendary rarity (supply 1) is appropriate when buying multiples would be degenerate or pointless. The design principle is: if having 4 of a unit would be broken, or if having even 1 is often pointless, supply 1 is used to enforce the intended single-copy use case.
author: elyot
context: Design discussion about what makes a unit legendary, with elyot explaining the philosophy using examples.

insight: Vai Mauronax is supply 1 (Legendary) because an earlier smaller cheaper version with similar freeze mechanics at supply 4 was a nightmare to calculate whether you could breach. Making it a one-of resolved the calculation complexity while preserving the interesting design.
author: elyot
context: Elyot explaining the design history of Vai Mauronax in the context of discussing supply limits for new units.

insight: Pivot (a proposed mechanic giving P1 a one-time-use convert 1G into GG) was found to balance many sets containing green units, reducing P1/P2 asymmetry without making any game more play-advantaged for P1 in testing. Nearly any set with a green unit becomes more balanced with Pivot present.
author: masn6811
context: Post-event assessment of the Pivot mechanic tested during the November 2020 event.

insight: After the November 2020 test event, masn assessed that Pivot, Smorcus nerf, Vore buff, Iso Kronus HP buff, and Centurion cost increase to 20GGBBR were all ready for ranked ladder immediately, while Felid and Thorium Dynamo needed more testing before ladder inclusion.
author: masn6811
context: Comprehensive post-event unit assessment by masn covering all new and patched units from the November 2020 test event.

insight: Units granted by spells (e.g., Blood Pact granting prompt Husks, Arms Race granting buildtime-2 Tarsiers) are buildtime 1 by default unless the spell card explicitly states otherwise. Granted units also do not trigger 'when bought' effects.
author: randomflyingtaco
context: Question about rules for spell-granted units, inferred from reading Blood Pact and Arms Race card text.

insight: Centrifuge (Fuge) at 9 gold (reverting from the 5-gold version) is considered more fun and less degenerate than the cheap version, which had a skewed gold-to-resource ratio that made it a strong combo piece with other skew-ratio units but bad when used normally. The old expensive version was safer in the current meta because large combo units like 16BBB Drake had been toned down.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of Centrifuge (Fuge) balance in the context of upcoming patch changes during the November 2020 test event.
